coredata.dc:title: Sub-5 nm amorphous iridium oxide nanosheets with synergistic multi-element doping for enhanced acidic oxygen evolution electrocatalysis

coredata.dc:description: Acidic oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is crucial for developing proton exchange membrane water electrolyzers, which has been hindered by the high overpotential of iridium oxide (IrO x )-based catalysts and the scarcity of iridium. Herein, we introduce a novel strategy to synthesize ultra-thin amorphous IrO x nanosheets with flexible mono/multi-element doping. Benefiting from synergistic W/Mo/Co co-doping effect, WMoCo-IrO x nanosheets (WMC-IrO x NS) sample optimally modulates the Ir electronic structure, maintains the neutral oxygen vacancy concentration and stabilizes Ir sites. Additionally, the nanosheets expose abundant Ir active centers. WMC-IrO x NS exhibits superior OER electrocatalysis performance with a low overpotential of 241 mV, excellent stability of 275 h at 10 mA cm−2, and a high mass activity of 545.4 A/g Ir. The catalyst follows the lattice oxygen mechanism (LOM) pathway. As observed in in situ attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, compared to the un-doped sample, WMC-IrO x NS accumulates negligible Ir–O–O* intermediates. Therefore, through the multi-element synergistic co-doping effect, the catalyst weakens the adsorption energy of O–O* intermediates and promotes O2 release, thereby lowering OER energy barriers in acidic conditions. This work opens an inspiring avenue for designing IrO x -based catalysts with high activity and low Ir usage.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of M−IrO x NS.

Structural characterizations of WMC-IrO x NS. (a, b) AFM image and the thickness profile of WMC-IrO x NS. (c, d) TEM image and HR-TEM image of WMC-IrO x NS. The inset in (c) is the SAED image of WMC-IrO x NS. (e, f) XRD patterns and peak shift of WMC-IrO x NS and other samples, i.e. M−IrO x NS (M = W/Mo/Co) and IrO x NS.

Element distribution of M−IrO x NS (M = W/Mo/Co) and WMC-IrO x NS. (a-c) HAADF-STEM images of M−IrO x NS and the corresponding STEM-EDS elemental mapping images of Ir, M and O. (d) HAADF-STEM image of WMC-IrO x NS and the corresponding STEM-EDS elemental mapping images of Ir, W, Mo, Co and O.

XPS spectra analysis of WMC-IrO x NS, M−IrO x NS (M = W/Mo/Co) and IrO x NS. (a) Ir 4f XPS spectra. (b, c) O 1 s XPS spectra and comparison of oxygen vacancy ratios of WMC-IrO x NS, M−IrO x NS and IrO x NS. (d-f) W 4f, Mo 3d, Co 2p XPS spectra of WMC-IrO x NS and M−IrO x NS. (g) The different valence states content of W, Mo, Co elements in WMC-IrO x NS and M−IrO x NS.

The electrocatalytic OER performance of WMC-IrO x NS and other prepared samples in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. (a, b) LSV curves and corresponding overpotentials at 10 and 100 mA cm−2. (c, d) Tafel plots and EIS plots. (e, f) The jmass curves and comparison of specific jmass at 1.55 V (vs. RHE). (g) Comparison between WMC-IrO x NS and previously reported catalysts in terms of overpotential and jmass at 1.55 V (vs. RHE). (h) Chronoamperometry measurements at 10 mA cm−2.

In situ ATR-FTIR analysis of WMC-IrO x NS and IrO x NS. (a) Schematic illustration of in situ ATR-FTIR measurement device. (b, c) In situ ATR-FTIR spectra recorded in the potential range of 1.1–1.8 V (vs. RHE) for WMC-IrO x NS (b) and IrO x NS (c). (d, e) Schematic illustration of OER catalytic mechanism for WMC-IrO x NS and IrO x NS, and the reason of enhanced catalytic activity of WMC-IrO x NS compared to IrO x NS.

head.ce:title: Sub-5 nm amorphous iridium oxide nanosheets with synergistic multi-element doping for enhanced acidic oxygen evolution electrocatalysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Acidic oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is crucial for developing proton exchange membrane water electrolyzers, which has been hindered by the high overpotential of iridium oxide (IrO x )-based catalysts and the scarcity of iridium. Herein, we introduce a novel strategy to synthesize ultra-thin amorphous IrO x nanosheets with flexible mono/multi-element doping. Benefiting from synergistic W/Mo/Co co-doping effect, WMoCo-IrO x nanosheets (WMC-IrO x NS) sample optimally modulates the Ir electronic structure, maintains the neutral oxygen vacancy concentration and stabilizes Ir sites. Additionally, the nanosheets expose abundant Ir active centers. WMC-IrO x NS exhibits superior OER electrocatalysis performance with a low overpotential of 241 mV, excellent stability of 275 h at 10 mA cm−2, and a high mass activity of 545.4 A/g Ir. The catalyst follows the lattice oxygen mechanism (LOM) pathway. As observed in in situ attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, compared to the un-doped sample, WMC-IrO x NS accumulates negligible Ir–O–O* intermediates. Therefore, through the multi-element synergistic co-doping effect, the catalyst weakens the adsorption energy of O–O* intermediates and promotes O2 release, thereby lowering OER energy barriers in acidic conditions. This work opens an inspiring avenue for designing IrO x -based catalysts with high activity and low Ir usage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Arising from the energy crisis, many efforts have been made to develop and utilize renewable energy sources such as solar and wind energy [1]. Using these power sources for electrochemical water splitting to produce green hydrogen is a promising strategy to address the intermittency of renewable energy [2,3]. Among the innovative pathways, proton exchange membrane electrolyzers (PEM) operating under acidic conditions is particularly well-suited [4,5]. Nevertheless, the slow kinetics of the four-step electron transfer reactions in oxygen evolution reaction (OER) leads to higher energy costs, substantially impeding the development of PEM [6,7]. Among the various acidic OER electrocatalysts developed, iridium oxide (IrO x ) is a particularly promising for practical applications [8]. Given the limited intrinsic activity and stability of IrO x
[8] and the extremely high cost of precious Ir metal [6], it becomes significant to optimize the catalytic performance of IrO x and reduce Ir usage.

Due to the high cost of catalysts, the design strategies often require consideration to maximize the utilization of active sites [9], including single atom sites design [10–12], morphology regulation [11,13,14], etc. In particular, morphology engineering for synthesizing low-dimensional IrO x -based materials presents an effective approach to address this challenge [15]. Current studies on low-dimensional IrO x -based materials primarily focus on one-dimensional (1D) nanoribbons [16,17], 1D nanorods [18], and two-dimensional (2D) nanosheets [15,19,20], etc. Among these, catalysts with 2D lamellar structure are highly desirable for OER, where the reactions predominantly occur on the surface [21]. Owing to the high specific surface area of 2D nanostructures, the atomic utilization can be improved, thus providing more surface active centers and facilitating faster electron transfer to optimize the reaction kinetics [15,20,21]. Since the non-intrinsic layered structure of IrO x makes forming nanosheets challenging [22], it is highly significant to develop universal methods for synthesizing 2D IrO x -based catalysts.

Apart from active sites exposure, numerous strategies have been employed to enhance the intrinsic catalytic properties of active sites in IrO x , including hetero-element doping [8,23–25], crystal phase engineering [15,16,26], strain engineering [8,27,28], and acid-resistant support effect [18,29], etc. Among these, hetero-element doping is considered a promising and rational method to boost the catalytic performance of IrO x and decrease Ir usage. Compared to single-element doping, multi-element co-doping provides a broader design space for adjusting elemental composition and electronic structure, as well as optimizing the binding energy of oxygen-containing intermediates with active sites [30]. However, due to the varying intrinsic properties of metallic elements (e.g., atomic radius, electronegativity, binding force with oxygen) [31], it is challenging to achieve uniform doping from mono- to multi-element and to avoid phase separation. Although several studies have reported the preparation of element-doped IrO x
[8,17,25,28], a universal applicable single-phase-controlled synthesis approach remains scarce, let alone further accessibility in low dimensional nanostructured morphology.

In this study, we propose a novel approach to fabricate ultra-thin amorphous IrO x nanosheets with flexible mono/multi-element doping. Leveraging the synergistic W/Mo/Co co-doping effect, WMoCo-IrO x nanosheets (WMC-IrO x NS) catalyst achieves optimal modulation of Ir electronic structure, maintains the neutral oxygen vacancy concentration, and stabilizes Ir actives centers. Furthermore, the nanosheets with high specific surface area expose abundant Ir active sites, thereby enhancing the catalytic activity. WMC-IrO x NS exhibits a low overpotential of 241 mV and excellent stability of 275 h at 10 mA cm−2 in 0.5 M H2SO4. WMC-IrO x NS also demonstrates an impressive mass activity of 545.4 A/g Ir, outperforming most previously reported Ir-based electrocatalysts. In situ attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) results confirm the origin of the high catalytic activity. Through the multi-element synergistic co-doping effect, WMC-IrO x NS accumulates negligible Ir–O–O* intermediates during the reaction process and efficiently promotes O2 release, thus lowering the overall OER energy barriers. Our study provides an effective method for designing element-doped amorphous IrO x nanosheets electrocatalysts that achieve remarkable catalytic performance for acidic OER.

Iridium (III) chloride (IrCl3, 99.9 %), Ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate ((NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O, 99 %), Cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O, 99 %) were purchased from HEOWNS. Ammonium metatungstate hydrate ((NH4)6H2W12O40·xH2O, 99.5 %) was purchased from Meryer Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. Citrate (C6H8O7, 99.5 %), NafionTM perfluorinated resin solution (Nafion 117 solution, ∼5% in a mixture of lower aliphatic alcohols and water) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.8 %) was purchased from Shanghai Yien Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. Dicyandiamide (C2H4N4) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Iridium(IV) oxide (IrO2, 99.9 %) was purchased from Bide Pharmatech Co., Ltd. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 95–98 %), Ethanol (C2H5OH, A.R.) were purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd.

30 mmol of C6H8O7 and 30 mmol of C2H4N4 were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water and ultrasonicated for 1 h to obtain a colorless and clear solution, and then the mixed solution was transferred to a 50 mL hydrothermal kettle and reacted at 180 °C for 6 h to obtain a brown-yellow solution, which was then filtered and dried in an oven at 100 °C to produce brown-yellow powder as CDs

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of IrWMoCo-C, IrM-C (M = W/Mo/Co) and Ir-C
ce:section.ce:para: 0.028 mmol IrCl3, 0.0003 mmol (NH4)6H2W12O40·xH2O, 0.0005 mmol (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O, 0.0032 mmol CoCl2·6H2O, 200 mg CDs and 800 mg NaCl were dissolved in 45 mL deionized water and stirred for 1 h to form the homogeneous solution. The molar ratio of Ir: W: Mo: Co was 4: 0.5: 0.5: 0.5, respectively. The solution was then freeze-dried. The obtained sample was calcined under Ar atmosphere at 400°C with a heating rate of 5°C min−1 and then cooled to room temperature. After that, the product was washed by filtration with abundant deionized water to remove NaCl template and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 3 h to produce IrWMoCo-C. The synthesis process of IrM-C (M = W/Mo/Co) was similar to the IrWMoCo-C except the molar ratio of Ir: M (M = W, Mo, Co) was 4: 1.5, respectively. The synthesis process of Ir-C was similar to the IrWMoCo-C, except that no doping elements were introduced and 0.038 mmol IrCl3 was directly added

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of WMC-IrO x NS, M−IrO x NS (M = W/Mo/Co) and IrO x NS
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained IrWMoCo-C powder was annealed in air at 400 °C with a heating rate of 5°C min−1 for 5 h to produce WMC-IrO x NS. Similarly, IrM-C (M = W/Mo/Co) and Ir-C were annealed under the same conditions, resulting in the formation of M−IrO x NS (M = W/Mo/Co) and IrO x NS, respectively.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were performed using Rigaku SmartLab with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) at 40 kV and 50 mA. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were captured by Dimension ICON with Being CSPM5500. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns were recorded by Jeol JEM-F200. The aberration-corrected high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) images, the corresponding energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (STEM-EDS) elemental mapping images and TEM-EDS spectra were collected by Jeol JEM-ARM200F. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on the Kratos Axis Supra spectrometer. The inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) data were collected from Agilent 5110. The Raman spectra were obtained by Raman XploRA Plus (HORIBA Scientific).

The electrocatalytic measurements were conducted on a standard three-electrode system on a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua, China) with a carbon paper supported samples as the working electrode, platinum sheet as the counter electrode, saturated mercurous sulfate electrode (MSE) as the reference electrode, and 0.5 M H2SO4 solution as the acid electrolyte. Typically, 1.0 mg catalyst was dispersed in a solution containing 188 μL of ethanol and 12 μL of 5 wt% Nafion-117 solution by sonication for 30 min to form homogeneous catalyst ink. Subsequently, 19.6 μL well-dispersed catalyst ink was carefully dropped onto the carbon paper (0.5 × 0.5 cm) with drying naturally for testing. All the experiments were carried out at room temperature. Before evaluating OER catalytic activity in 0.5 M H2SO4, the working electrode was scanned from 1.15 to 1.85 V at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1, until the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves were stable. Thereafter, linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves with an 85 % iR correction were measured at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. All potentials were referenced to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) using the equation ERHE = EMSE + 0.652 + 0.0591 × pH = EMSE + 0.652. Tafel plots were calculated by formula η = blogj + a, where b was the Tafel slope. All the data were obtained from LSV curves. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was obtained open circuit voltage in the frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz. The mass activity (jmass) of the electrocatalysts was calculated according to following equation,
j
m a s s

j
g e o

A
g e o

n o b l e
m e t a l
where mnoble metal is the calculated noble metal mass loaded onto a carbon paper electrode. For the prepared catalysts, Ir was considered to be noble metal. jgeo is the geometric current density from LSV curves and Ageo is the area of the working electrode, which is 0.25 cm2.

The electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl) of the electrocatalysts was collected by measuring the CV curves at different scan rates from 20, 40, 60, 80 to 120 mV s−1. The potential range was selected from 1.24 to 1.34 V, which is a non-faradaic region for each sample. Cdl was obtained from the slope of the graph by plotting the difference between the anodic and cathodic current at 1.29 V against the scan rates.

Chronopotentiometry measurements were conducted at 10 mA cm−2 to evaluate stability for the prepared electrocatalysts. 32.6 μL of well-dispersed catalyst ink was carefully dropped onto the carbon paper (0.5 × 0.5 cm) and dried naturally to serve as the working electrode for stability testing. The others remained unchanged.

All in situ ATR-FTIR data were collected at INVENIO S (BRUKER). An IR spectrometer, a KBr beam splitter, an MCT detector were used. The custom-fabricated IR electrochemical cell was a three-electrode system, in which Ag/AgCl and platinum wire were used as the reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. Catalyst was dropped on an Si ATR wafer, which dried naturally and acted as the working electrode. The electrolyte was 0.5 M H2SO4 solution and circulated through a peristaltic pump. The measurement width of the infrared spectra is 800 to 4000 cm−1, and the spectral resolution is 4 cm−1. The background spectrum of the working electrode was recorded under the open circuit potential condition. The recorded spectra were atmosphere compensated and smoothed by the OPUS software.

A schematic illustration for the synthesis of element-doped IrO x nanosheets (M−IrO x NS) is shown in Fig. 1 . Initially, carbon quantum dots (CDs) were prepared. XRD pattern of CDs exhibits a broad peak at 23.4° ( Figure S1 ) [32], which is ascribed to graphite (002) plane [33]. TEM image displays that the average particle size of spherical nanoparticles is below 10 nm ( Figure S2 ). Additionally, luminescenece under UV light was observed for the CDs ( Figure S3 ) [34,35], confirming the successful preparation of CDs. Futhermore, XPS of the C 1s high-resolution spectrum reveals three fitting peaks at 284.8, 286.4, and 288.7 eV, attributed to C–C, C–O/C–N, and O–CO functional groups in CDs, respectively ( Figure S4 ) [36]. It has been proven that CDs with abundant functional groups could offer bonding sites with metal ions featuring empty d-orbitals to form structurally stable complexes [37,38]. Cosequently, the precursor IrM@CDs composites loaded on NaCl were obtained by uniformly mixing the reactants and then freeze-drying. This step is essential for introducing various metallic elements at the atomic level and ensuring uniform distribution. Followed by pyrolysis under Ar atmosphere, IrM-C@NaCl was produced. Utilizing the stable cubic morphology of NaCl template, the process mainly involved the crosslinking and self-assembly of CDs [39,40], which transformed the well-dispersed IrM@CDs into a coating layer on NaCl. After pyrolysis, Ir and M elements were embedded in atomic states within the carbon layer. Upon removing the NaCl template, the original coating layer collapsed to 2D nanosheets, resulting in the formation of IrM-C. This step establishes the foundation for the layered structure in the final product. Subsequently, IrM-C was annealed in air to remove the carbon matrix support, inheriting the pre-existing lamellar structure and converting it into M−IrO x NS.

To demonstrate the versatility of this method, transition metals with distinctly different properties were employed for mono/multi-element doping. Specifically, we selected the early transition metals from different periods, including W (5d), Mo (4d) and Co (3d). After doping, the lamellar structures of the intermediate products, IrWMoCo-C and IrM-C (M = W/Mo/Co), were firstly characterized. Taking IrW-C as a example, TEM images reveal an uncrystallized layered structure ( Figure S5 ), which serve as the structural origin for the final products. XRD patterns of IrWMoCo-C, IrM-C and comparative un-doped sample Ir-C show only amorphous carbon peaks without detectable metal peaks ( Figure S6 ) [41]. These indicate that in the synthesized intermediate products, Ir and M atomically disperse on the carbon layer and subsequently convert into M−IrO x NS through oxidation at atomic-level diffusion. Based on these results, the structures of WMC-IrO x NS and M−IrO x NS (M = W/Mo/Co), were further analyzed. The lamellar structure of WMC-IrO x NS is clearly observed in the AFM image (Fig. 2
a), and the thickness of the sheets is ∼4.7 nm (Fig. 2 b). TEM image also dispalys the morphology of ultra-thin wrinkled nanosheets (Fig. 2 c). The diffuse halos and absence of diffraction spots in SAED pattern indicate the amorphous structure (Fig. 2 c inset). Additionally, HR-TEM image displays no obvious lattice fringes (Fig. 2 d). Similarly, TEM images of M−IrO x NS and un-doped IrO x sample (IrO x NS) demonstrate the analogous nanosheet structures, with the inset SAED patterns showing diffuse diffraction rings that confirm the amorphous nature ( Figure S7-
10). HR-TEM images of these samples also lack discernible lattice fringes ( Figure S7-
10). Overall, it is evidenced that the synthesis route depicted in Fig. 1 effectively produces ultra-thin amorphous nanosheets with mono- to multi-element doping.

Furthermore, XRD patterns of WMC-IrO x NS, M−IrO x NS (M = W/Mo/Co), and IrO x NS all display two broad humps at approximately 35° and 65°, which differ from the standard PDF card (PDF#43-1019) of crystalline phase IrO2 (Fig. 2 e). These humps are attributed to the amorphous IrO x
[42–44], which supports the conclusion that the produced samples exhibit amorphous structures. Meanwhile, the absence of discernible peaks for doping elements confirms the successful incorporation into the materials. Notably, the peak positions of these doped samples show different shift degrees compared to IrO x NS (Fig. 2 f). These offsets are closely correlated with the structural distortions due to the difference in atomic radius between Ir and doping elements [45–47]. Specifically, since the atomic radius of W and Mo are slightly larger than that of Ir, the peak positions of W-IrO x NS, Mo-IrO x NS shift downward towards lower diffraction angles [46]. Conversely, the smaller atomic radius of Co causes an upward shift in the peak position of Co-IrO x NS [48]. Moreover, the position of the spectral peak of WMC-IrO x NS differs from those of other samples, indicating that co-doping effect of W/Mo/Co leads to restructuring of this material. HAADF-STEM and the corresponding STEM-EDS elemental mapping were also performed for M−IrO x NS and WMC-IrO x NS (Fig. 3 ). As shown in Fig. 3 a-c, Ir, M (M = W/Mo/Co) and O elements are homogeneously distributed within M−IrO x NS, revealing the successful doping of three different elements into IrO x nanosheets. Similarly, the uniformly presence of Ir, W, Mo, Co, and O elements (Fig. 3 d) demonstrates the effective co-doping of the three elements into the IrO x nanosheets. TEM-EDS spectra ( Figure S11-
14) and the ratios of elemental content from ICP-OES ( Table S1 ), further confirm the presence of all doping elements. Additionally, HAADF-STEM image, STEM-EDS elemental mapping images, TEM-EDS spectrum and ICP-OES results of IrO x NS also suggest the successful preparation of un-doped amorphous IrO x nanosheets ( Figure S15-
16,
Table S1
). Due to the short-range ordered structure of amorphous materials, achieving a homogeneous elements distribution remains a significant challenge [49]. However, the special synthesis route shown in Fig. 1 can effectively address this problem, producing amorphous IrO x nanosheets with uniformly distributed doping elements and enabling the precise control over the types and quantities of these elements.

XPS was employed to investigate the valence states and electronic structure of the elements in the synthesized samples. The full-scan XPS spectra confirm the presence of Ir, O and doping elements ( Figure S17 ), indicating the successful introduction of W, Mo, Co into WMC-IrO x NS and M−IrO x NS (M = W/Mo/Co). The high-resolution Ir 4f XPS spectra of WMC-IrO x NS, M−IrO x NS and IrO x NS show two sets of doublets with binding energies of ∼ 62.2 and ∼ 65.2 eV, ∼ 63.2 and ∼ 66.2 eV, respectively, implying the co-existence of Ir3+ and Ir4+ (Fig. 4
a) [23,27]. Notably, using Ir 4f XPS spectrum of IrO x NS as the benchmark, the introduction of W, Mo, Co shifts the positions of these two sets of doublets in M−IrO x NS and WMC-IrO x NS, signifying the re-arrangement in the electronic structure of Ir within the doped samples [8]. The Ir 4f doublets of W-IrO x NS and Mo-IrO x NS shift positively, indicating higher valence states of Ir [19]. In contrast, Co-IrO x NS, shifts the doublets negatively, suggesting the lower valence state of Ir [8]. Notably, the Ir 4f peak positions of WMC-IrO x NS differ from those of M−IrO x NS, demonstrating that the electronic structure of Ir active sites is re-tuned through the co-doping effect of W/Mo/Co. Since the electronic structure of Ir can affect the catalytic activity in acidic OER process [6,7], it is anticipated that the prepared doped samples are expected to display different catalytic properties.

As shown in Fig. 4 b, O 1 s XPS spectra of all products can be deconvoluted into three peaks, which are lattice oxygen (Lattice O) at ∼ 530.0 eV, coordinatively unsaturated oxygen or oxygen in hydroxyl group (Ads. OH) at ∼531.4 eV, and the oxygen in adsorbed water (Ads·H2O) at ∼ 533.0 eV [24]. The peak fitting data of O 1s XPS signals for all samples are summarized in Table S2 . Generally, the persence of Ads. OH in XPS peak fitting is generally associated with the formation of the oxygen vacancies (Ov), which induce the unsaturated coordination of Ir as active sites, thereby improving OER catalytic performance [50]. And several previous studies have indeed reported that the percentage of Ads. OH fitting peak area qualitatively reflects the relative Ov concentration [51,52]. Consequently, as analyzed for all electrocatalysts in Fig. 4 c, M−IrO x NS (M = W/Mo/Co) and WMC-IrO x NS exhibit the relative Ov concentrations of 40.23 %, 43.62 %, 33.46 %, and 34.01 %, respectively, all of which exceed that of IrO x NS (31.76 %). Moreover, to further confirm the magnitude of the relative Ov concentrations, Raman spectra were collected for all samples ( Figure S18 ). All samples show only Eg modes (typical Ir–O vibrational peak) at ∼540 cm−1, corresponding to the short-range ordered structure of amorphous IrO x NS [53,54]. The weaker intensity of the Ir–O vibrational peak is often an important characteristic of disordered materials and increased Ov [51,55]. As shown in Figure S18 , Mo-IrO x NS exhibits the weakest peak intensity of Eg modes peak, representing the highest relative Ov concentration, followed by W-IrO x NS, WMC-IrO x NS, Co-IrO x NS, and IrO x NS, demonstrating consistency with the O 1s XPS peak fitting results. These results indicate that different types and quantities of doping elements have varying abilities to augment the number of Ir active sites by boosting the Ov concentration, thereby enhancing the catalytic performance compared with un-doped sample IrO x NS.

XPS spectra of W 4f, Mo 3d, and Co 2p in WMC-IrO x NS and M−IrO x NS (M = W/Mo/Co) were also fitted and analyzed independently. In the W 4f spectra (Fig. 4 d), WMC-IrO x NS exhibits a single pair of doublets with binding energies of 34.8 and 37.0 eV, attributed to the presence of WV. In contrast, W-IrO x NS displays two sets of fitting peaks with binding energies of 34.9 and 37.1 eV, 35.6 and 37.8 eV, corresponding to WV and WVI, respectively [56]. Mo 3d XPS spectra presented in Fig. 4 e show that both WMC-IrO x NS and Mo-IrO x NS exhibit two sets of doublets at ∼232.0 and ∼235.1 eV, ∼232.7 and ∼235.8 eV, representing MoV and MoVI, respectively [57]. Similarly, Co 2p XPS spectra in Fig. 4 f reveal two pairs of fitting peaks in WMC-IrO x NS and Co-IrO x NS, corresponding to CoIII and CoII with binding energies of ∼779.6 and ∼794.8 eV, ∼781.1 eV and ∼796.5 eV, respectively [58]. The valence states of W, Mo, Co elements in WMC-IrO x NS and M−IrO x NS are summarized in Fig. 4 g. These results indicate that, compared to M−IrO x NS, the valence states of W, Mo, Co elements in WMC-IrO x NS also change. In summary, the changes in valence states, together with the preceding analyses, provide a deeper understanding of the conclusions drawn from Fig. 4 c. According to the charge conservation law in defect chemistry [25,59], the incorporation of different valence cations in IrO x will lead to changes in valence state of Ir or the Ov formation [59]. In theory, the introduction of high-valence cations into IrO x nanosheets (e.g., W-IrO x NS, Mo-IrO x NS) leads to a negative shift in the binding energies of Ir 4f XPS spectra, while doping with low valence cations (e.g., Co-IrO x NS) results in a positive shift [25]. However, the results shown in Fig. 4 a are contrary to this theoretical expectation, suggesting that the doping elements facilitate the Ov formation [25,59], which is consistent with the conclusions in Fig. 4 c. These findings confirm that mono/multi-element doping effects with high and/or low valence cations can modulate the electronic structure of Ir and promote the Ov formation to a certain extent. Combined with the high specific surface area resulted from the ultra-thin nanosheets morphology [20,21], the catalysts also effectively expose a sufficient number of Ir active sites. Consequently, all doped samples could significantly adjust energy barriers of the four-step electron transfer reactions, resulting in improved catalytic performance during the OER process.

The OER catalytic performance of WMC-IrO x NS and M−IrO x NS (M = W/Mo/Co) were evaluated in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. IrO x NS and commercial iridium oxide (Com. IrO2) were measured as the comparative references to investigate the effects of mono/multi-element doping in acidic OER electrocatalysis. Among them, WMC-IrO x NS exhibits the superior catalytic activity according to the LSV curves after iR correction and geometric area normalization (Fig. 5
a), delivering a significantly higher current density than other samples at the same potential. The overpotential of WMC-IrO x NS to reach 10 mA cm−2 is 241 mV, which is much lower than that of W-IrO x NS (260 mV), Mo-IrO x NS (257 mV), Co-IrO x NS (261 mV), IrO x NS (266 mV) and Com. IrO2 (372 mV) (Fig. 5 b). Additionally, the overpotentials of each catalyst at 100 mA cm−2 also were recorded. WMC-IrO x NS still exhibits the lowest overpotential (315 mV), followed by W-IrO x NS (342 mV), Mo-IrO x NS (342 mV), Co-IrO x NS (344 mV), IrO x NS (349 mV), and Com. IrO2 (567 mV). The Tafel plots of electrocatalysts exhibit slopes of 50.9, 54.3, 53.0, 54.9, 55.7 and 112.6 mV dec-1 for WMC-IrO x NS, W-IrO x NS, Mo-IrO x NS, Co-IrO x NS, IrO x NS and Com. IrO2, respectively (Fig. 5 c). The considerably smaller slope obtained by WMC-IrO x NS catalyst suggests a significantly accelerated kinetics during acidic OER. Meanwhile, the charge transfer was analyzed using EIS. Nyquist plots (Fig. 5 d) are fitted by an equivalent circuit in Figure S19 to quantify the charge transfer resistance (Rct) ( Table S3 ). WMC-IrO x NS and M−IrO x NS have lower Rct compared to IrO x NS, and Com. IrO2, with WMC-IrO x NS exhibiting the lowest Rct. This indicates that the introduction of W, Mo, Co elements into IrO x nanosheets accelerates electron transport during OER process. These results demonstrate that elemental doping effect improves the catalytic activity of the samples, and multi-element synergistic co-doping effect leads to superior performance compared to mono-element doping in M−IrO x NS. Based on the conclusions from Fig. 4 and the catalytic activity of doped samples, it is evident that rational modulation of the electronic structure of Ir active sites achieves the optimal catalytic activity for WMC-IrO x NS. Furthermore, WMC-IrO x NS has the neutral Ov concentration among all samples due to synergistic co-doping with W/Mo/Co. It has been demonstrated that increasing Ov concentration within a certain range can enhance catalytic activity, while the excessive Ov concentration can induce inhibitory effects [60]. Therefore, both the optimal modulation of Ir electronic structure and the neutral Ov concentration of WMC-IrO x NS contribute to the improvement of OER catalytic activity.

To assess the jmass of the prepared electrocatalysts, the jmass polarization curves were calculated based on the mass of Ir loaded on carbon paper and LSV curves (Fig. 5 a), which were presented in Fig. 5 e. The jmass of WMC-IrO x NS is significantly higher than that of M−IrO x NS (M = W/Mo/Co), IrO x NS, and Com. IrO2 at the corresponding potential. Due to the scarcity and high cost of Ir, the jmass becomes a crucial factor for practical applications. The jmass at 1.55 V (vs. RHE) of WMC-IrO x NS is 545.4 A gIr−1, which is ∼1.8, ∼2.2, ∼2.2, ∼2.1 and ∼41.0 times higher than that of W-IrO x NS (307.9 gIr −1), Mo-IrO x NS (253.0 gIr −1), Co-IrO x NS (248.2 gIr −1), IrO x NS (258.5 gIr −1) and Com. IrO2 (13.3 gIr −1), respectively (Fig. 5 f). This demonstrates that both the jmass and overpotential increase with multi-element doping in WMC-IrO x NS. Furthermore, WMC-IrO x NS displays competitive electrochemical properties in terms of catalytic activity and the jmass at 1.55 V (vs. RHE) compared to other representative Ir-based OER electrocatalysts in acidic media (Fig. 5 g and
Table S4 ), such as element-doped IrO x , 2D IrO x -based nanosheets, other low-dimensional IrO2, Ir-based alloy@IrO x -based composites, and perovskite-based materials. In general, catalysts with higher jmass tend to exhibit a larger electrochemically active surface area (ECSA). Since a higher jmass reflects more available Ir active sites for reactions, thus enhancing the overall catalytic efficiency. Consequently, the Cdl values of all the prepared samples were estimated using CV curves measured at different scan rates to evaluate the ECSA ( Figure S20
a-f). The most active WMC-IrO x NS catalyst exhibits the highest Cdl value of 59.8 mF cm−2, followed by M−IrO x NS, IrO x NS, and Com.IrO2 ( Figure S20
g). The 2D nanosheets morphology leads to an increase in the ECSA of all the synthesized amorphous IrO x nanosheets catalysts, indicating that the regulation of 2D morphology is beneficial for the catalytic activity. Combined with multi-element doping, WMC-IrO x NS achieves the maximum ECSA. Overall, through mono/multi-element doping effect and the 2D morphology regulation strategy, synthesized samples exhibit remarkable jmass and higher ECSA, highlighting the significant advantage in decreasing the usage of Ir.

Electrocatalytic stability is also a crucial parameter for acidic OER process due to the anodic potential and corrosive electrolyte. To evaluate the stability of the electrocatalysts, chronoamperometry measurements were conducted with the prepared samples loaded on carbon paper as the working electrodes in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution at 10 mA cm−2, as depicted in Fig. 5 h. WMC-IrO x NS demonstrates the highest OER stability, exhibiting approximately 100 mV increase after continuous working for 275 h. In contrast, IrO x NS shows a noticeable potential rise of over 100 mV after only 125h of operation. Regarding the stability of M−IrO x NS (M = W/Mo/Co), as illustrated in Figure S21 , single element-doped samples also suppress Ir dissolution and enhance the anti-corrosive effects of IrO x nanosheets compared to IrO x NS, resulting in moderate stability improvements in W-IrOx NS (135 h), Mo-IrO x NS (235 h), Co-IrO x NS (215 h). However, the stability of WMC-IrO x NS catalyst is particularly noteworthy, indicating that co-doping with W/Mo/Co provides a more stabilized structure of IrO x nanosheets under acidic OER conditions. Additionally, Com. IrO2 exhibits a sharp potential increase after only 40 h of operation, attributed to the high applied potential required to achieve the desired current density and the poorer stability in acidic media. Although synthesized IrO x NS shows better stability than that of Com. IrO2, the catalyst still degrades rapidly due to the absence of selected metal elements that could stabilize Ir active sites, further highlighting the importance of elements doping. These results underscore that single and multiple element-doped amorphous IrO x nanosheets, as achieved through the synthesis method in Fig. 1, are conducive to stabilizing the IrO x structure and effectively alleviating the dissolution of Ir active sites, thus improving the stability in acid media. Notably, the WMC-IrO x NS electrocatalyst, produced by multi-element synergistic co-doping, exhibits lower decay and superior catalytic stability in acidic OER compared to M−IrO x NS.

To experimentally investigate the acidic OER catalytic process of WMC-IrO x NS and elucidate the origin of the excellent catalytic activity, in situ ATR-FTIR were performed to track the changes in the surface reaction intermediates [61]. The catalyst ink was uniformly dispersed on a Si crystal and then assembled into the device shown in Fig. 6
a for testing. Initially, in situ ATR-FTIR results of WMC-IrO x NS were obtained at different applied potentials, ranging from open circuit potential (OCP) to 1.8 V ( Figure S22
a and Fig. 6 b). At OCP, no discernible absorption peak is observed within the range of 1000 to 1400 cm−1. As the potential increases to 1.2 V, a new absorption band emerges at the range of 1070–1150 cm−1, indicating the presence of O–O bonds [62,63]. If the broad spectral peak corresponds to the surface oxygen-containing intermediates on the Ir active sites, the increases in overpotential subject these adsorbed species to large electric fields which should cause a frequency shift [63]. However, the absence of stark tuning suggests that this broad and featureless spectral peak is not attributable to the adsorbed Ir–O–O* intermediates, which may be due to the superoxide dissolved in solution. Therefore, it can be demonstrated that WMC-IrO x NS catalyst leads to negligible accumulation of O–O* intermediates during the acidic OER electrocatalysis process, thus accelerating the acidic OER kinetics. For comparison, in situ ATR-FTIR measurements were also conducted for IrO x NS under the same applied conditions. Similar to WMC-IrO x NS, IrO x NS show no significant spectral peak at OCP, and as the potential increases to a relatively low value of 1.1 V, the spectral peak of O–O bonds appears at the range of 1070–1150 cm−1 ( Figure S22
b and Fig. 6 c). Differing from WMC-IrO x NS, in the acidic OER process, the spectral peak of IrO x NS exhibits a frequency dependent shift with increasing potential, indicating that this spectral peak can be attributable to O–O* intermediates adsorbed on the Ir active sites of the catalyst surface. Notably, as the applied potential increases to 1.8 V, the peak intensity of IrO x NS increases rapidly, representing the severe accumulation of Ir–O–O* intermediates in IrO x NS [64]. The excessive accumulation of Ir–O–O* intermediates hinders the efficient operation of the four-step electron transfer reactions in acidic OER of IrO x NS. Therefore, WMC-IrO x NS catalyst can effectively regulate the accumulation of oxygen-containing intermediates over Ir active sites through multi-element synergistic co-doping effect to enhance the OER catalytic activity in acidic conditions.

The intrinsic properties of amorphous IrO x indicate that the electrocatalysts predominantly follow the lattice oxygen mechanism (LOM) pathway during the acidic OER process [7,65]. The detailed four-step electronic reactions are shown in Fig. 6 d. The process consists of four steps, beginning with the dehydrogenation of Ir–OH to Ir–O, followed by the formation of Ir–O–OH, subsequent dehydrogenation to Ir–O–O, and finally, release of O2 and the regeneration of Ir–OH. The cycle then continues. Combined with the appearance of Ir–O–O* intermediates in in situ ATR-FTIR measurements and the specific steps of LOM pathway, the rate determining step (RDS) of acidic OER process is the desorption of O–O* species over Ir active sites to form O2. The illustration in Fig. 6 e explains the origin of the intrinsically high OER catalytic activity of WMC-IrO x NS. As shown in Fig. 6 b-c and
Figure S22 , WMC-IrO x NS accumulates fewer Ir–O–O* intermediates than IrO x NS, demonstrating that the adsorption energy of the O–O* intermediates on Ir active sites of WMC-IrO x NS catalyst is lower than that of IrO x NS. Resultantly, O–O* species are more susceptible to desorb from Ir active sites, thus promoting the generation of the final product O2, and decreaseing the energy barrier of the RDS. It has been revealed that the OER catalytic activity can be predicted by the adsorption free energy differences among the oxygen-containing intermediates, which are closely related to the electronic structure of Ir active sites [6,7]. Therefore, through synergistic co-doping effect of W/Mo/Co, WMC-IrO x NS catalyst optimally modulates the electronic structure of Ir active sites while maintaining the neutral Ov concentration, thereby improving both the adsorption and desorption energy barriers of the RDS and enabling a more efficient and rapid four electron OER process in acidic media.

In summary, we present a novel and universal method for synthesizing ultra-thin amorphous IrO x nanosheets with mono/multi-element doping. Benefiting from the co-doping of W/Mo/Co, WMC-IrO x NS catalyst re-modulates the electronic structure of Ir, achieves the neutral Ov concentration and stabilizes Ir actives centers. Additionally, the high specific surface area of the nanosheets exposes abundant active centers. As employed for acidic OER electrocatalysis, the catalyst demonstrates superior catalytic performance with an overpotential of 241 mV and stability for 275 h at 10 mA cm−2. Furthermore, the jmass at 1.55 V (vs. RHE) is 545.4 A/g Ir, surpassing most reported Ir-based electrocatalysts. The catalyst primarily follows LOM pathway during the OER process, and in situ ATR-FTIR results reveal that WMC-IrO x NS catalyst cause negligible accumulation of O–O* intermediates compared to IrO x NS. Therefore, through the multi-element synergistic co-doping effect, WMC-IrO x NS attenuates the adsorption energy of O–O* species over Ir active sites and boosts the release of O2, thereby lowering the OER energy barriers. Our work establishes an effective design principle for the rational activity and stability of IrO x -based electrocatalysts.
